revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[as:চিন্তামণি]][[bn:চিন্তামণি]][[bpy:চিন্তামনি]][[ca:Cintamani]][[de:Chintamani]][[en:Cintamani]][[eo:Ĉintamanio]][[es:Chintamani]][[fr:Chintamani]][[hi:चिन्तामणि]][[id:Cintamani]][[it:Cintamani]][[ja:如意宝珠]][[ka:ჩინტამანი]][[kn:ಚಿಂತಾಮಣಿ (ರತ್ನ)]][[ko:여의보주]][[ml:ചിന്താമണി രത്നം]][[ms:Cintamani]][[no:Chintamani]][[pl:Ćintamani]][[pt:Pedra Chintamani]][[ro:Cintamani]][[ru:Чинтамани]][[te:చింతామణి (రత్నం)]][[th:จินตามณี]][[uk:Чінтамані]][[vi:Cintamani]][[zh:如意寶珠]]


{{Short description|Wish-fulfilling mythical jewel within Dharmic traditions|qq=}}
{{About||Hindu Chintamani Ganesha shrine in Ashtavinayak temples|Chintamani Temple, Theur|City of India|Chintamani, Karnataka|qq=}}
[[File:Statua di kichijote, IX-X sec..JPG|thumb|572x572px|Japanese depiction of {{ill|Lakshmi|en|Lakshmi|qid=Q131443|lt=Lakshmi|1=Lakshmi}} ([[Kisshōten|Kichijote]]), bearing the Cintamani]]
'''Cintāmaṇi''' ({{ill|Sanskrit|en|Sanskrit|qid=Q11059|lt=Sanskrit|1=Sanskrit}}; {{ill|Devanagari|en|Devanagari|qid=Q38592|lt=Devanagari|1=Devanagari}}: {{lang|sa|चिन्तामणि|qq=}}; {{lang-zh|c=如意寶珠|p=Rúyì bǎozhū|qq=}}; {{ill|Korean language|en|Korean language|qid=Q9176|lt=Korean|1=Korean language}}: 여의보주/yeouiboju; Japanese {{ill|Romanization of Japanese|en|Romanization of Japanese|qid=Q192509|lt=Romaji|1=Romanization of Japanese}}: {{lang|ja|Nyoihōju|qq=}}), also spelled as '''Chintamani''' (or the ''Chintamani Stone''), is a {{ill|Wish|en|Wish|qid=Q241625|lt=Wish|1=Wish}}-fulfilling jewel resembling a pearl described in {{ill|Hindus|en|Hindus|qid=Q10090|lt=Hindus|1=Hindus}} and {{ill|Mahayana|en|Mahayana|qid=Q48362|lt=Mahayana|1=Mahayana}} {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|qid=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}} traditions. It is one of several {{ill|Mani Jewel|en|Mani Jewel|qid=Q24906547|lt=Mani Jewel|1=Mani Jewel}} images found in [[:en:Buddhist scripture|Buddhist scripture]].
Within Hinduism, it is connected with the gods {{ill|Vishnu|en|Vishnu|qid=Q11380|lt=Vishnu|1=Vishnu}} and {{ill|Ganesha|en|Ganesha|qid=Q1579|lt=Ganesha|1=Ganesha}}. In Hindu tradition, it is often depicted as a fabulous {{ill|Gemstone|en|Gemstone|qid=Q83437|lt=jewel|1=Gemstone}} in the possession of Vishnu as the {{ill|Kaustubha|en|Kaustubha|qid=Q393413|lt=Kaustubha|1=Kaustubha}} Mani or as on the forehead of the [[Nāga|Naga]] king called as Naga Mani, or on the forehead of the [[:en:Makara (Hindu mythology)|Makara]].{{Citation needed|date=May 2011|qq=}} The ''[[:en:Yoga Vasistha|Yoga Vasistha]]'', originally written in the 10th century CE, contains a story about the cintamani.<ref>{{cite book |last=Venkatesananda |first=Swami |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1FFdOj2dv8cC |title=The Concise Yoga Vāsiṣṭha |publisher=State University of 11 York Press |year=1984 |isbn=0-87395-955-8 |location=Albany |pages=346–353 |oclc=11044869 |author-link=Swami Venkatesananda|qq=}}</ref>
The Hindu Vishnu Purana speaks of the "{{ill|Syamantaka|en|Syamantaka|qid=Q7659453|lt=Syamanta jewel|1=Syamantaka}}, bestowing prosperity upon its owner, encapsulates the Yadu clan system".<ref>The Past before us: Historical traditions of early North India, Romila Thapar, Harvard, 2013</ref> The Vishnu Purana is attributed to the mid-first millennium CE.
In Buddhism, it is held by the {{ill|Bodhisattva|en|Bodhisattva|qid=Q178149|lt=Bodhisattvas|1=Bodhisattva}} (divine beings with great compassion, wisdom and power) [[:en:Avalokiteshvara|Avalokiteshvara]] and {{ill|Kṣitigarbha|en|Kṣitigarbha|qid=Q604687|lt=Kṣitigarbha|1=Kṣitigarbha}}. It is also seen carried upon the back of the [[:en:Wind horse|Lung Ta]] (wind horse) which is depicted on Tibetan [[Prayer flag#Symbols and prayers|prayer flags]]. By reciting the {{ill|Dharani|en|Dharani|qid=Q1207590|lt=Dharani|1=Dharani}} (small hymn) of Cintamani, Buddhist tradition maintains that one attains the Wisdom of Buddha, able to understand the truth of the Buddha, and turn afflictions into {{ill|Bodhi|en|Bodhi|qid=Q502029|lt=Bodhi|1=Bodhi}}. It is said to allow one to see the Holy {{ill|Retinue|en|Retinue|qid=Q694166|lt=Retinue|1=Retinue}} of [[Amitābha]] and assembly upon one's deathbed.  In Tibetan Buddhist tradition the Chintamani is sometimes depicted as a luminous pearl and is in the possession of several of different forms of the Buddha.<ref>R. A. Donkin, [https://books.google.com/books?id=leHFqMQ9mw8C&dq=Cintamani&pg=PA179 ''Beyond price: pearls and pearl-fishing : origins to the Age of Discoveries''], American Philosophical Society, 1998. {{ISBN|978-0-87169-224-5|qq=}}. p. 170</ref>
In Japan, where the {{ill|Hindus|en|Hindus|qid=Q10090|lt=Hindus|1=Hindus}} goddess ''{{ill|Lakshmi|en|Lakshmi|qid=Q131443|lt=Lakshmi|1=Lakshmi}}'' is known as ''[[Kisshōten]]'' in [[Shinto]], she is commonly depicted with a Cintāmaṇi in her hand.
==Etymology==
'''Cintāmaṇi''' (Sanskrit; Devanagari: चिन्तामणि): 'Wish-Fulfilling Gem' ({{bo|t=ཡིད་བཞིན་ནོར་བུ|w=yid bzhin norbu|qq=}})<ref>Scheidegger, Daniel (2009). 'The First Four Themes of Klong chen pa's ''Tsig don bcu gcig pa''.' Achard, Jean-Luke (director) (2009). [http://himalaya.socanth.cam.ac.uk/collections/journals/ret/pdf/ret_16.pdf ''Revue d'Etudes Tibetaines'']. April 2009. p.49</ref> The ''mani'' (jewel) is translated in {{ill|Chinese language|en|Chinese language|qid=Q7850|lt=Chinese|1=Chinese language}} ''ruyi'' or ''ruyizhu'' 如意珠 "as-one-wishes jewel" or ''ruyibaozhu'' 如意寶珠 "as-one-wishes precious jewel". ''Ruyibaozhu'' is pronounced in {{ill|Japanese language|en|Japanese language|qid=Q5287|lt=Japanese|1=Japanese language}} ''nyoi-hōju'' or ''nyoi-hōshu'' 如意宝珠. ''Ruyizhu'' is pronounced in {{ill|Korean language|en|Korean language|qid=Q9176|lt=Korean|1=Korean language}} ''yeouiju'' 여의주.
== Hinduism ==
The {{ill|Brihad Bhagavatamrita|en|Brihad Bhagavatamrita|qid=Q4096625|lt=Brihad Bhagavatamrita|1=Brihad Bhagavatamrita}} compares this gem with devotion towards Vishnu:
{{Blockquote|text=स-प्रेमका भक्तिर् अतीव-दुर्लभा स्वर्गादि-भोगः सुलभोऽभवश् च सः ।
चिन्तामणिः सर्व-जनैर् न लभ्यते लभ्येत काचादि कदापि हातकम् ॥ २३२ ॥
sa-premakā bhaktir atīva-durlabhā svargādi-bhogaḥ sulabho'bhavaś ca saḥ {{!|qq=}}
cintāmaṇiḥ sarva-janair na labhyate labhyeta kācādi kadāpi hātakam {{!|qq=}}{{!|qq=}} 232 {{!|qq=}}{{!|qq=}}
Anyone can obtain a piece of glass and sometimes a piece of gold, but not everyone can obtain a cintāmaṇi because it is most rare. Similarly, the pleasures of heaven and liberation are easily achieved, but prema-bhakti is extremely rare.|title={{ill|Brihad Bhagavatamrita|en|Brihad Bhagavatamrita|qid=Q4096625|lt=Brihad Bhagavatamrita|1=Brihad Bhagavatamrita}}|source=Verse 2.4.232|qq=}}
The {{ill|Bhagavata Purana|en|Bhagavata Purana|qid=Q682958|lt=Bhagavata Purana|1=Bhagavata Purana}} remarks that the cintamani may give worldly pleasures and affluence in {{ill|Svarga|en|Svarga|qid=Q2725577|lt=Svarga|1=Svarga}}, but spiritual advancement allows one to attain {{ill|Vaikuntha|en|Vaikuntha|qid=Q127570|lt=Vaikuntha|1=Vaikuntha}}, the realm of Vishnu that is difficult to achieve, even for ''yogins''.<ref>{{Cite web |last=www.wisdomlib.org |date=2022-05-29 |title=Nārada Meets Bhakti (Devotion in a Human Form) [Chapter 1] |url=https://www.wisdomlib.org/hinduism/book/the-bhagavata-purana/d/doc1113128.html#note-t-185576 |access-date=2022-08-12 |website=www.wisdomlib.org |language=en|qq=}}</ref>{{Hinduism small|qq=}}
==Buddhism==
[[File:Goryeo-Kshitigarbha (Chijang)-late.14c.jpg|right|thumb|14th century {{ill|Goryeo|en|Goryeo|qid=Q28208|lt=Goryeo|1=Goryeo}} painting of {{ill|Kṣitigarbha|en|Kṣitigarbha|qid=Q604687|lt=Kṣitigarbha|1=Kṣitigarbha}} holding a ''cintamani'']]
[[File:ManiStone.jpg|left|thumb|{{ill|Mani stone|en|Mani stone|qid=Q1650169|lt=Mani stone|1=Mani stone}}]]In Buddhism, the wish fulfilling jewel (Skt. ''{{IAST|maṇi|qq=}}'', ''{{IAST|cintā-maṇi|qq=}}'', ''{{IAST|cintāmaṇi-ratna|qq=}}'') is an important mythic symbol indicating a magical jewel that manifests one's wishes, including the curing of disease, purification of water, granting clothing, food, treasure etc. It is a common symbol for the teachings and qualities of the Buddha.<ref>[http://www.buddhism-dict.net/ddb/ "ruyizhu", in Digital Dictionary of Buddhism]</ref> 
One of the manifestation of Avalokitesvara is named [[Cintāmaṇicakra]] and holds a Cintāmaṇi.
In {{ill|Tibetan Buddhism|en|Tibetan Buddhism|qid=Q483889|lt=Tibetan Buddhism|1=Tibetan Buddhism}} the Cintāmaṇi is said to be one of four {{ill|Relic|en|Relic|qid=Q187616|lt=Relic|1=Relic}}s that came in a chest that fell from the sky (many [[:en:Terma (Buddhism)|terma]] fell from the sky in caskets) during the reign of king Lha {{ill|Thothori Nyantsen|en|Thothori Nyantsen|qid=Q887022|lt=Thothori Nyantsen|1=Thothori Nyantsen}} of Tibet.{{Citation needed|date=August 2007|qq=}} Though the king did not understand the purpose of the objects, he kept them in a position of reverence. Several years later, two mysterious strangers appeared at the court of the king, explaining the four relics, which included the Buddha's bowl (possibly a [[:en:Singing Bowl|Singing Bowl]]) and a {{ill|Mani stone|en|Mani stone|qid=Q1650169|lt=Mani stone|1=Mani stone}} with the [[:en:Om Mani Padme Hum|Om Mani Padme Hum]] {{ill|Mantra|en|Mantra|qid=Q131510|lt=Mantra|1=Mantra}} inscribed on it. These few objects were the bringers of the {{ill|Dharma|en|Dharma|qid=Q134293|lt=Dharma|1=Dharma}} to {{ill|Tibet|en|Tibet|qid=Q17252|lt=Tibet|1=Tibet}}.
The {{ill|Kintamani, Bali|en|Kintamani, Bali|qid=Q39173|lt=Kintamani|1=Kintamani, Bali}} mountainous region in {{ill|Bali|en|Bali|qid=Q3125978|lt=Bali|1=Bali}} was named after the Cintamani.
== Perennialism ==
{{ill|René Guénon|en|René Guénon|qid=Q496850|lt=René Guénon|1=René Guénon}} considered the Cintamani to be the equivalent of the {{ill|Philosopher's stone|en|Philosopher's stone|qid=Q182053|lt=Philosopher's stone|1=Philosopher's stone}}.<ref>{{ill|René Guénon|en|René Guénon|qid=Q496850|lt=Guénon, René|1=René Guénon}} (2004) [1962]. ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=qEQQht2Y2CQC Symbols of Sacred Science]''. Sophia Perennis, USA. {{ISBN|0-900588-78-0|qq=}}. p. 277</ref>
{{clear|qq=}}
